title: Geophysical and Environmental Sensors
description: This experiential course will provide students an introduction to sensors, microcontrollers, automation, data collection and programming from the perspective of sensing the Earth and the environment. Requisites: Prereq: MATH 252Z ; ERTH 363 or CS 122 .

title: Earth and Environmental Data Analysis
description: Tools-based instruction in data analysis for earth and environmental scientists. Topics include descriptive statistics, visualization, uncertainty analysis, hypothesis testing, regression, time series, and directional data. Requisites: Prereq: MATH 246 or MATH 251Z .

title: Geocommunication
description: Scientific writing and presentations for the geological sciences. Focus on writing scientific papers and proposals, preparing oral and visual presentations.

title: Introduction to Space Physics
description: Course explores the interaction of the solar wind with the Earth's magnetosphere using fundamental plasma physics supported and motivated by spacecraft observations. Students will gain an understanding of the physics governing the interaction building from single particle plasma motion to specific observation supported examples. Requisites: Prereq: PHYS 253 , MATH 282 . Equivalent to: PHYS 423M

title: Vertebrate Paleontology
description: Evolution of vertebrates, including ourselves, based on fossil evidence. Physical and other evolutionary constraints are addressed, and lab exercises provide practical experience. Requisites: Prereq: ERTH 103 or ERTH 203 .

title: Paleoecology and Functional Morphology
description: Ecological methods for the study of fossil organisms, both terrestrial and marine. Covers a range of methods from those that reconstruct the ecology of individual species to those that deal with whole communities and ecosystems. Laboratory offers practical and analytical experience in the methods. Requisites: Prereq: one from ERTH 103 , ERTH 203 , BI 223Z .

title: Hillslope Geomorphology
description: Hillslope processes and landforms; includes hillslope hydrology, overland flow erosion, weathering and soil formation, soil creep, landslides and related hazards, glacial and periglacial processes, effects of land-use practices and fire, and landscape evolution.

title: Hydrogeology
description: Study of the origin, motion, and physical and chemical properties of ground water. Emphasizes quantitative analysis of flow and interaction with geologic materials. Requisites: Prereq: CH 222Z or CH 225H ; ERTH 316 .

title: Tectonics
description: Tectonic processes and examples. Global kinematics of plates and the forces that drive them. Continental deformation in compressional, shear, and extensional settings. Requisites: Prereq: ERTH 350 and calculus.

title: Fluid Dynamics
description: Introduction to the continuum theory of fluid dynamics, focusing on the Navier-Stokes equations of motion including common simplified limits and extensions. Applications are drawn from Earth and Planetary Science, Biology, and Physics. Requisites: Prereq: PHYS 252 , MATH 252Z .

title: Mechanical Earth
description: Introduction to continuum mechanics. Includes stress and strain, friction, elasticity, viscous fluids, constitutive laws, equations of motion, and deformation of the Earth. Requisites: Prereq: ERTH 315 or PHYS 202 ; MATH 256 .

title: Signal Processing
description: A theoretical and hands-on introduction to signal processing techniques that are widely used in geophysical, geological, and related fields. Requisites: Prereq: MATH 252Z or ERTH 363 .

title: Introduction to Seismology
description: Introduction to observational, theoretical, and computational seismology. Includes review of earth structure, source representation, ray theory, and seismic wave phenomena. Requisites: Prereq: MATH 256 , ERTH 455 .

title: Thermodynamic Geochemistry
description: Introduction to geologic application of classical chemical thermodynamics. Gibbs free energy and its temperature, pressure, and composition derivatives; fugacity, activity, and chemical potential. Solutions, ideal and nonideal. Requisites: Prereq: ERTH 311 or ERTH 332 ; CH 223Z ; MATH 253Z .

title: Isotope Geochemistry
description: Introduction to nuclear physics and isotope systematics; techniques of isotope analysis; applications of stable and radioactive isotopes in geochronology and as tracers of geological processes.

title: Soil and Environmental Chemistry
description: Understanding the flow and cycling of chemicals in soils is vital for addressing many pressing societal issues, including mitigating climate change, growing abundant and safe food, and protecting water quality. This class will describe fundamental soil chemical principles and consider their broader applications. Requisites: Prereq: ENVS 477 , CH 222Z .

title: Volcanology
description: Products and processes of volcanism, transport of magma in the conduit and into the atmosphere, eruptive mechanisms, volcanic hazards. Requisites: Prereq: ERTH 201 ; PHYS 201 or PHYS 251 ; MATH 251Z or MATH 246 .

description: Introduction to continuum mechanics. Includes stress and strain, friction, elasticity, viscous fluids, constitutive laws, equations of motion, and deformation of the earth.

title: Signal Processing
description: A theoretical and hands-on introduction to signal processing techniques that are widely used in geophysical, geological, and related fields.

title: Introduction to Seismology
description: Introduction to observational, theoretical, and computational seismology. Includes review of earth structure, source representation, ray theory, and seismic wave phenomena.

title: Isotope Geochemistry
description: Introduction to nuclear physics and isotope systematics; techniques of isotope analysis; applications of stable and radioactive isotopes in geochronology and as tracers of geological processes.

title: Soil and Environmental Chemistry
description: Understanding the flow and cycling of chemicals in soils is vital for addressing many pressing societal issues, including mitigating climate change, growing abundant and safe food, and protecting water quality. This class will describe fundamental soil chemical principles and consider their broader applications.

title: Volcanology
description: Products and processes of volcanism, transport of magma in the conduit and into the atmosphere, eruptive mechanisms, volcanic hazards.

description: This course is an introduction to the academic field of Ethnic Studies, the interdisciplinary, comparative and relational study of race, ethnicity and indigeneity in the United States. description: Focuses on historical, cultural, and social issues in Asian America and surveys scholarship in Asian American studies.

title: Introduction to Chicanx and Latinx Studies

title: Introduction to Native American Studies

title: Introduction to Pacific Islander Studies

Intended to be taken by junior year to introduce students to the second half of the Ethnic Studies major. Required for Ethnic Studies majors. Offered once per year. Requisites: Prereq: ES 101 ; one from ES 250 , ES 252 , ES 254 , ES 256 , ES 258 .

title: Race and Popular Culture: [Topic]
description: Examines the interface between race and popular culture, surveying the historical development, political significance, and social influence ofpopular culture in the United States. Offered alternate years. Repeatable 4 times for a maximum of 20 credits when topic changes

title: Indigenous Peoples of Oregon
description: This course examines the history, culture, social and political issues of the Indigenous peoples of Oregon through oral traditions, primary sources, and secondary sources, focused on the major themes of sovereignty, traditional knowledge, and decolonization.

title: Women of Color Issues and Concerns
description: Contemporary social issues and feminism among women of color in the United States. Requisites: Prereq: ES 101 recommended.

title: Native Americans and the Environment
description: Critical issues in Native American environmentalism.

title: Social Equity and Criminal Justice
description: Critical issues related to police, prisons, criminal justice, and racial and gender inequalities.

title: Environmental Racism
description: Explores environmental justice as both a field of scholarship and organizing framework that links power, justice, and inequality to environmental issues. Special attention is given to the specific forms of racism which produce environmental injustice.

title: Black Sexual Politics
description: Explores the gender and sexuality politics that influence the social, political, economic and cultural development of black communities in the diaspora, including the United States.

title: Race, Ethnicity, and Cinema: [Topic]
description: Examines the history and politics of race, ethnicity, and indigeneity in relation to film, including questions of production, distribution, and reception, in the United States. Offered alternate years. Repeatable 4 times for a maximum of 20 credits when topic changes

title: Race, Migration, and Rights
description: Examines historical and contemporary politics in race, immigration, and migration.

description: Examines issues and problems confronting children and families in the United States. Issues such as disability, poverty, health care, addictions, racism, and violence are addressed.

description: Explores the historic basis and current design of family and human services. Emphasizes services to children, youth, adults, and families.

title: Diversity in Human Services
description: This course is designed to provide the foundational knowledge, awareness, and skills needed for working with diverse populations in the human services. This course will explore issues of equity and inclusion across the levels of the ecological model.

title: Human Development in the Family Context
description: Examines human development within the context of the family from an evidence-based perspective. Integration of contemporary family issues experienced across the lifespan within the context of the human service profession. Previously taught as FHS 328. Requisites: Prereq: FHS 215 .

description: This course prepares students with the knowledge and skills necessary for writing competently for professional/scientific audiences through a series of assigned readings, in- and out-of-class assignments, and quizzes. Students will practice iterative writing by giving and receiving peer reviews and incorporating peer and instructor feedback. Requisites: Prereq: WR 121Z , FHS 215 .

title: Youth Psychopathology in Context
description: Presents child and adolescent psychopathology and problems within a diagnostic framework. Topics address psychosocial issues for youth in family and cultural contexts. Students cannot receive credit for both BEHT 320 and FHS 329 . Requisites: Prereq: FHS 215 . Equivalent to: BEHT 320

title: Individual Interventions in Ecological Contexts
description: Introduces the concept of individual interventions within an ecological model. Provides knowledge and development of basic listening skills and how to apply these skills to individuals in diverse contexts. Requisites: Prereq: FHS 215 , FHS 216 , FHS 301 .

title: Group and Community Interventions
description: Introduces the concept of group intervention within the context of group theory and community development. Provides knowledge and development of group interventions, including group facilitation skills and curriculum development. Requisites: Prereq: FHS 330 .

description: This class provides students skills in case management and human service focused direct practice with adolescents and adults, including needs assessments, case conceptualization, service plan development, and direct service intervention. Requisites: Prereq: FHS 331 , FHS 471 .

description: This course explores and explodes cultural stereotypes while examining some of the social and cultural phenomena that many associate most strongly with France. Topics may include language and identity; love and romance; food and fashion; wars and revolutions; and cultural imperialism.

Topics include history, geography, demographics, foodways, social movements, art, politics, and other cultural phenomena. Sequence with FR 202 , FR 203 . Requisites: Prereq: FR 103 or FR 112 recommended.

title: Intermediate French II
description: Development of French language proficiency beyond beginning level through the study of the cultural products, practices, and perspectives of the Francophone world (France, Belgium, Switzerland, Canada, Caribbean, Lebanon, Magreb, Senegal, etc.). description: Training in language and cultures of the French-speaking world using literary texts, newspapers, short stories, poetry, films, websites, videos. Grammar review and vocabulary enrichment. Conducted in French. Requisites: Prereq: FR 203 .

title: Oral Skills

title: French Survey of Francophone Literature
description: Introduction to major authors and texts of the French-speaking world outside of France. Exploration of history and culture of former French colonies. Requisites: Prereq: FR 301 or FR 302 .

title: Knights, Queens, and Rebels
description: Introduction to major themes and ideas in French cultural and textual production before the 17th century. Requisites: Prereq: FR 301 or FR 302 .

title: Monarchy, Liberty, Revolution
description: Introduction to major themes and ideas in French literature from the 17th and 18th centuries through the reading of representative texts. Requisites: Prereq: FR 301 or FR 302 .

title: Modernity and Creativity
description: Representative literary works from the 19th-21st centuries with attention to textual analysis and connections to politics and society. Requisites: Prereq: FR 301 or FR 302 .

title: French Poetry
description: French and Francophone poems from the Middle Ages to the 21st century, literary movements, introduction to textual analysis and modern critical approaches. Requisites: Prereq: FR 301 , FR 302 .

title: French Theater
description: Explores important aspects of French theater. Reading plays from different periods. Emphasizes formal aspects and critical reading. Requisites: Prereq: FR 301 , FR 302 .

title: French Narrative
description: Covers important aspects of French and Francophone narrative. Reading texts from different periods. Emphasis on formal aspects and critical reading. Requisites: Prereq: FR 301 , FR 302 .

title: French Film
description: Focuses on the differences between American culture and French and Francophone cultures. Addresses a sensitive issue exemplified by the attitude of the international movie industry. Requisites: Prereq: FR 301 , FR 302 .

title: The Natural Environment
description: The earth's physical landscapes, vegetation patterns, weather, and climate; emphasis on the dynamic interactions among climate, landforms, vegetation, and soils.

title: The Human Planet
description: The spatial organization of humans and their activities on Earth’s surface. Cultural, political, and economic influences shaping places and their interconnections.

title: Our Digital Earth
description: Explores the emergence and use of the geospatial technologies that are shaping contemporary society and are pervasive in our everyday lives.

title: Field Studies: [Topic].  1-2 Credits.

title: World Regional Geography
description: Introduction to the world's cultural regions. Study of the cultural and environmental factors that make different parts of the world distinct.

title: Geography of Europe
description: Physical and cultural processes that have shaped the rural and urban landscapes of Europe.

title: Geography of the United States and Canada
description: Historical and geographical analysis of the physical and human geography of the U.S. and Canada. Topics include physical regions, settlement patterns, economic development, and urbanization. Offered alternate years.

title: Geography of the Middle East and North Africa
description: Physical and cultural processes that have shaped the rural and urban landscapes of the Middle East and North Africa.

title: Geographies of Outdoor Adventure
description: Introduces cultural geography by tracing the emergence and contemporary significance of outdoor adventure sports. Their global development since the late 1800s coincides with changing ideas about nature, wilderness, and the utility of play, and reflects modern social disparities and environmental challenges.

title: The World and Big Data
description: Explores technical foundations and social and economic applications of big data along the “5V” dimensions of volume, variety, velocity, veracity, and visualization.

title: Climatology
description: Energy and moisture in the atmosphere, atmospheric circulation, controls of regional and microclimates, applied climatology, climatic variations, past and future climates. Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 141 .

title: Geomorphology
description: Landforming processes with emphasis on mass movements, rivers, eolian, glacial, and coastal processes. Special fee. Requisites: Prereq: one from GEOG 141 , ERTH 102 , ERTH 202 .

title: Biogeography
description: The spatial patterns of biological diversity, and its causes, both in the present and in the past, and its relevance during a time of increasing human impact and climate change. Requisites: Prereq: one from GEOG 141 , ERTH 103 , ERTH 203 , BI 370 .

title: Population and Environment
description: Patterns of population growth over history and place, current policies and programs, and impacts and trends in United States and international contexts. Includes method and theory.

title: Geography of Globalization
description: Historical and geographical dimensions of globalization; emphasizes economic and social factors. Topics include multinationals, trade agreements, sustainability, global inequalities, and racial and gender divisions of labor.

title: Society, Culture, and Place
description: Examines ways in which geographical context reflects and shapes cultural and social processes. Importance of place and territory in human affairs.

title: Watershed Science and Policy
description: Physical and biological processes of watersheds; problems of land use, water quality, riparian zones, aquatic ecology; scientific basis of watershed management and policy. Special fee. Requisites: Prereq: one from GEOG 141 , ERTH 102 , ERTH 202 , BI 130 , BI 223Z .

title: Global Environmental Change
description: Natural and human-induced changes in the Earth System and their impact on different environmental systems. Not available to those who have taken GEOG 143. Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 141 .

title: Social Science Inquiry and Research
description: Understanding scientific inquiry, the scientific method and learning to critique social science research. Readings and discussion focus on the questions, methods, conclusions and outcomes of research.

title: Data Driven Cartography
description: Introduction to concepts of cartography through representing, organizing, analyzing, and visually presenting geospatial information and data on maps. In this course we will explore different types of map design, how to represent different data types on maps, and how data impacts visual display. Sequence with GEOG 481 , GEOG 492 , GEOG 493 . Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 181 or GEOG 481 .

title: Advanced Climatology: [Topic]
description: Topics in climatology, including physical climatology, dynamic and synoptic climatology, and paleoclimatology. Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 321 . Repeatable 99 times when topic changes

title: Advanced Biogeography: [Topic]
description: Selected topics in biogeography including relation of plants and animals to their environment, historical changes in plant distribution, and palynological analysis. Special fee. Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 323 . Repeatable 99 times when topic changes

title: Hydrology and Water Resources
description: Emphasis on surface water including precipitation, evapotranspiration, surface runoff, and stream flow. Understanding and analysis of processes. Management for water supply and quality. Special fee. Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 321 or GEOG 322 ; MATH 111Z .

title: Fluvial Geomorphology
description: Hydraulics and hydrology of stream channels; channel morphology and processes; drainage network development; fluvial deposits and landforms; field and analytical methods. Required field trips. Special fee. Requisites: Prereq: MATH 112Z ; one from GEOG 322 , GEOG 425 , ERTH 334 .

title: Long-Term Environmental Change
description: Evolution of the physical landscape during the Quaternary period. Elements of paleoclimatology, paleoecology, and geomorphology. Required field trips. Special fee. Requisites: Prereq: one from GEOG 321 , GEOG 322 , GEOG 323 .

title: Fire and Natural Disturbances
description: Wildfire and other landscape disturbance processes, historical and current patterns of fire, use and management of fire.  Offered alternate years. Requisites: Prereq: one from BI 307 , GEOG 323 , BI 370 .

title: Political Geography
description: Spatial perspectives on global political patterns and processes. Relationship of political territories to resources, ethnic patterns, and ideological communities. Impact of political arrangements on identity, conflict, and peace.

title: Urban Geography
description: Urbanization throughout the world, the structure of urban settlements; cities as regional centers, physical places, and homes for people; geographic problems in major urban environments.

title: Cultural Geography
description: Patterns of culture as a force in human affairs.  Dynamics of identity, place, and power.  The creation of culture at different scales.

title: Culture, Ethnicity, and Nationalism
description: Relationship of ethnic groups and nationality to landscapes, perception, and cultural geographic phenomena. Distribution of ethnic and national groups.  Junior standing required.

title: Tourism and Development
description: Tourism-related concepts and practices associated with tourism planning, development, marketing, and impacts in different geographic contexts.

title: Environment and Development
description: Critical analysis of development concepts. Economic activity and environmental impacts. Sustainable development. Development projects and landscapes in the industrializing world.

title: Contemporary Food Systems
description: Explores contemporary food systems at local, national, and global scales. Emphasis on the political economy and sociocultural dynamics linking agriculture, food industries, and consumption.

title: GIScience I
description: Introduction to geographic information science, geographic information systems (GIS), the current population survey (CPS), remote sensing, and cartography. Sequence with GEOG 482 , GEOG 491 .

title: GIScience II
description: Spatial data collection, spatial data models, database design, data editing, geographic information system (GIS) project management, and advanced topics in geographic information science. Sequence with GEOG 481 , GEOG 491 , GEOG 493 . Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 481 .

title: Remote Sensing I
description: Introduction to remote sensing science including its physical basis, instruments, platforms, data, processing methods, and applications. Sequence with GEOG 486 . Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 481 .

title: Remote Sensing II
description: The use of digital electromagnetic data for classification, mapping, and monitoring biologic, hydrologic, atmospheric, geologic, and human processes and environmental change. Sequence with GEOG 485 . Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 485 .

title: Mapping with Drones
description: This course introduces students to the understanding and applying the technologies, theories, and skills needed to acquire drone photography and convert this into maps useful in many professions. Applications include such subjects as resource management, physical environments, topographic mapping, vegetation, and urban characterization. Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 181 , GEOG 485 .

title: GIScience: [Topic]
description: Advanced topics on geographic information systems science including spatial analysis and modeling, data visualization, cartography, volunteered geographic information, GIS programming. Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 481 . Repeatable 5 times for a maximum of 24 credits

title: Advanced Geographic Information Systems
description: Automated processing of geospatial data using Python scripting language. Programming for spatial analysis such as 3-D and network modeling; processing of big data (satellite imagery, climate data, demographic information). Sequence with GEOG 481 , GEOG 482 . Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 482 .

title: Web Mapping
description: The web is full of maps, from providing context to local and international news to sharing research. We’ll explore interactive web mapping, including theories and principles of web mapping, fundamentals of cartography, and user interaction. Students will gather spatial data, develop a story or theme from their data, and produce interactive maps using front-end development. Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 481 .

title: Advanced Cartography and Geo-Visualization
description: This course explores advanced concepts in cartography, map design, geovisualization, and data visualization. Students will develop skills in GIS software and graphic design softwares to create a tailored final map design project with real-world data. Sequence with GEOG 393 . Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 393 .

title: Spatial Analysis
description: Introduction to a variety of spatial analysis techniques that can be used for understanding and modeling geographic phenomena. Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 481 .

title: Geographic Data Analysis
description: Analysis and display of geographical data by traditional data-analytical methods and by scientific-visualization approaches. Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 481 .

title: Qualitative Methods in Geography
description: Explores conceptual and practical dimensions of qualitative research. Includes linking theory and method; research question formulation; project design; ethics; data gathering, analysis, and presentation. Requisites: Prereq: one from GEOG 341 , GEOG 342 , GEOG 343 .

title: Urban Geography
description: Urbanization throughout the world, the structure of urban settlements; cities as regional centers, physical places, and homes for people; geographic problems in major urban environments.

title: Cultural Geography
description: Patterns of culture as a force in human affairs.  Dynamics of identity, place, and power.  The creation of culture at different scales.

title: Culture, Ethnicity, and Nationalism
description: Relationship of ethnic groups and nationality to landscapes, perception, and cultural geographic phenomena. Distribution of ethnic and national groups.

title: Tourism and Development
description: Tourism-related concepts and practices associated with tourism planning, development, marketing, and impacts in different geographic contexts.

title: Environment and Development
description: Critical analysis of development concepts. Economic activity and environmental impacts. Sustainable development. Development projects and landscapes in the industrializing world.

title: Contemporary Food Systems
description: Explores contemporary food systems at local, national, and global scales. Emphasis on the political economy and sociocultural dynamics linking agriculture, food industries, and consumption.

title: GIScience I
description: Introduction to geographic information science, geographic information systems (GIS), the current population survey (CPS), remote sensing, and cartography. Sequence with GEOG 582 , GEOG 591 .

title: GIScience II
description: Spatial data collection, spatial data models, database design, data editing, geographic information system (GIS) project management, and advanced topics in geographic information science. Sequence with GEOG 581 , GEOG 591 , GEOG 593 . Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 581 .

title: Remote Sensing I
description: Introduction to remote sensing science including its physical basis, instruments, platforms, data, processing methods, and applications. Sequence with GEOG 586 . Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 581 .

title: Remote Sensing II
description: The use of digital electromagnetic data for classification, mapping, and monitoring biologic, hydrologic, atmospheric, geologic, and human processes and environmental change. Sequence with GEOG 585 . Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 585 .

title: Mapping with Drones
description: This course introduces students to the understanding and applying the technologies, theories, and skills needed to acquire drone photography and convert this into maps useful in many professions. Applications include such subjects as resource management, physical environments, topographic mapping, vegetation, and urban characterization. Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 585 .

title: GIScience: [Topic]
description: Advanced topics on geographic information systems science including spatial analysis and modeling, data visualization, cartography, volunteered geographic information, GIS programming. Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 581 . Repeatable 5 times for a maximum of 24 credits

title: Advanced Geographic Information Systems
description: Socioeconomic analysis with geographic information systems (GIS) and the U.S. census, network modeling, 3-D models of natural and urban landscapes, web-based GIS and programming. Sequence with GEOG 581 , GEOG 582 . Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 582 .

title: Web Mapping
description: The web is full of maps, from providing context to local and international news to sharing research. We’ll explore interactive web mapping, including theories and principles of web mapping, fundamentals of cartography, and user interaction. Students will gather spatial data, develop a story or theme from their data, and produce interactive maps using front-end development.

title: Advanced Cartography and Geo-Visualization
description: This course explores advanced concepts in cartography, map design, geovisualization, and data visualization. Students will develop skills in GIS software and graphic design softwares to create a tailored final map design project with real-world data.

title: Spatial Analysis
description: Introduction to a variety of spatial analysis techniques that can be used for understanding and modeling geographic phenomena. Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 581 .

title: Geographic Data Analysis
description: Analysis and display of geographical data by traditional data-analytical methods and by scientific-visualization approaches. Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 581 .

title: Qualitative Methods in Geography
description: Explores conceptual and practical dimensions of qualitative research. Includes linking theory and method; research question formulation; project design; ethics; data gathering, analysis, and presentation.

title: Theory and Practice of Geography I
description: Introduction to professional practice in geography and the development of geographic concepts and theories from Ancient times through the mid-twentieth century.  Sequence with GEOG 612 , GEOG 613 .

title: Theory and Practice of Geography II
description: The development of geographic concepts and theories from the mid-20th century to the present. Students learn to refine effective geographic research questions. Sequence with GEOG 611 and GEOG 613 . Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 611 .

title: Research Design
description: Examines main components of research design, including research questions, methodological approach, institutional review boards, funding programs, proposal writing, and application. Sequence with GEOG 611 and GEOG 612 . Requisites: Prereq: GEOG 612 .

title: Progress in Physical Geography
description: Recent developments in climatology, geomorphology, hydrology, and biogeography. Lectures, readings, and presentation of faculty and student works in progress. Repeatable 11 times for a maximum of 12 credits

title: Progress in Human Geography
description: Recent developments in cultural, economic, environmental and political geography. Lectures, readings, and presentation of faculty and student works in progress. Repeatable 11 times for a maximum of 12 credits

title: Progress in Geographic Information Science
description: Recent developments in cartography, GIS, remote sensing, data analysis, and visualization. Lectures, readings, and presentation of faculty and student works in progress. Repeatable 11 times for a maximum of 12 credits

title: First-Year German
description: Provides a thorough grammatical foundation and an elementary reading knowledge of German as well as an understanding of the spoken language. Students cannot receive credit for both GER 101 and GER 141 . Sequence. Equivalent to: GER 141

title: First-Year German
description: Provides a thorough grammatical foundation and an elementary reading knowledge of German as well as an understanding of the spoken language. Students cannot receive credit for both GER 102 and GER 142 . Requisites: Prereq: GER 101 . Equivalent to: GER 142

title: First-Year German
description: Provides a thorough grammatical foundation and an elementary reading knowledge of German as well as an understanding of the spoken language. Requisites: Prereq: GER 102 .

title: German for Business I
description: Introduction to German language and culture to start building meaningful relationships and personal connections with peers and potential business partners on different career paths. Coursework includes interpreting authentic German language sources (reading/listening/viewing) and producing language in presentational and interpersonal modes of communication (oral and written discussions, presentations) in familiar, everyday contexts. Students cannot receive credit for both GER 141 and GER 101 . Sequence with GER 142 . Equivalent to: GER 101

title: German for Business II
description: Introduction to German language and culture to start building meaningful relationships and personal connections with peers and potential business partners on different career paths. Coursework includes interpreting authentic German language sources (reading/listening/viewing) and producing language in presentational and interpersonal modes of communication (oral and written discussions, presentations) in familiar, everyday contexts. Students cannot receive credit for both GER 142 and GER 102 . Sequence with GER 141 . Requisites: Prereq: GER 141 or GER 101 . Equivalent to: GER 102

description: A series of practical experiences on or off campus to understand principles or develop skills in performing selected tasks. Repeatable 99 times

title: Workshop: [Topic].  1-2 Credits.
description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery. Repeatable 99 times

title: Special Studies: [Topic].  1-5 Credits.
description: Experimental course - topic varies. Repeatable 99 times

title: Second-Year German I
description: Grammar and composition, reading selections from representative authors, conversation. Sequence. Requisites: Prereq: GER 103 or GER 105.

title: Second-Year German II
description: Grammar and composition, reading selections from representative authors, conversation. Requisites: Prereq: GER 201 .

title: Second-Year German III
description: Grammar and composition, reading selections from representative authors, conversation. Requisites: Prereq: GER 202 .

title: Existentialist Literature and Thought
description: Survey of the existential tradition in German and Scandinavian literature, philosophy, drama, and film. Historical and conceptual developments are considered, from Kierkegaard to Kafka.  Taught in English.  Multilisted with SCAN 220M . Equivalent to: SCAN 220M

title: Postwar Germany Nation Divided
description: Introduction to literary and cultural movements of public dissent, including 1960s student revolutions, in postwar Germany. Conducted in English.

title: Fascism and Culture
description: Key debates in German culture, including the adequate representation of the Holocaust, literature in society, and the roles of ethnic and gender identities within the nation. Conducted in English.

title: Germany: A Multicultural Society
description: Examines the multiethnic complexities of German, Austrian, and/or Swiss societies through the writings of African, Turkish, or Jewish Germans. Period of focus varies. Conducted in English.

title: The Culture of Money
description: Explores ideas about money, value, and exchange in German-speaking cultures from selected moments in modern history through readings of literature, philosophy, and the arts.

title: Sexuality
description: Examines discourses on sexuality (e.g., sexual norms, gender roles, and divergences from them) in modern German, Austrian, and Swiss-German contexts through literature, essays, and films.

title: War, Violence, Trauma
description: Examines war, violence, and trauma in German and Austrian cultural history, during the modern period, through works of literature, thought, art, music, and film.

title: Intermediate Language Training I
description: Extensive practice in speaking and writing German; complex grammatical structures in writing. Requisites: Prereq: GER 203 .

title: Intermediate Language Training II
description: Extensive practice in speaking and writing German; complex grammatical structures in writing. Requisites: Prereq: GER 311 .

title: Intermediate Language Training III
description: Extensive practice in speaking and writing German; complex grammatical structures in writing. Option during 313 to take the Zertifikat Deutsch exam. Requisites: Prereq: GER 312 .

title: Food, Culture, and Identity in Germany and Scandinavia
description: Examines the relationships among food, culture, and identity in Germany and Scandinavia. Interdisciplinary readings, lectures, films, and discussions focus on literary and symbolic representations of food from production to consumption, and on images and depictions of cooking, eating, drinking, and feasting. Multilisted with SCAN 345M . Equivalent to: SCAN 345M

title: Diversity in Germany
description: Examines the social construction of identity in German literature and culture. Addresses topics of plural voices and tolerance in German-speaking cultures. Topics vary. Conducted in English.

title: German Gender Studies
description: Student oral presentations and written papers on such topics as men and women writers of German romanticism, mothers and daughters in German literature, comparison of men and women dramatists. No knowledge of German required; readings and discussions in English.

title: German Cinema: History, Theory, Practice
description: In-depth analysis of various facets of German cinema. Topics include film and the Third Reich, cinema and technology, German filmmakers in American exile, German New Wave. Conducted in English.

title: German Fairy Tales
description: The German fairy tale in historical and theoretical context, from the Brothers Grimm and Romantic Tales to adaptations in the 20th and 21st Centuries. Taught in English.

title: Nature, Culture, and the Environment
description: Introduction to the contributions German writers, philosophers, scientists, and artists have made to changing notions of nature and its supposed opposition toculture.  Offered alternate years.

title: German Literature Poetry, Plays, Prose
description: Introduction to textual analysis--poetry, plays, and prose from 1800 to the present--in the context of major literary movements (romanticism, realism, modernism) and their social determinants. Focus on genre: poetry, plays, and prose. Requisites: Coreq: GER 311 .

title: German Literature Literary Movements
description: Introduction to textual analysis--poetry, plays, and prose from 1800 to the present--in the context of major literary movements (romanticism, realism, modernism) and their social determinants. Focus on literary movements. Requisites: Coreq: GER 311 .

title: German Literature Interpretive Models
description: Introduction to textual analysis--poetry, plays, and prose from 1800 to the present--in the context of major literary movements (romanticism, realism, modernism) and their social determinants. Focus on interpretive models. Requisites: Coreq: GER 311 .

title: Modern and Contemporary in Politics and Society
description: Examination of post-WWII cultural and social modernity. Most readings in Geman. Discussion, written assignments, and most reading assignments in German. Requisites: Pre- or coreq: GER 311 .

title: Works, Authors, Industries in Film and Media
description: Examines various aspects of German cinema and/or media in the context of socio-political history and aesthetics, including analysis of structure, composition, history, and artistry. Discussion, writing assignments, and most readings in German. Requisites: Pre- or coreq: GER 311 .

title: Music and the Arts
description: The course explores the history of German/Austrian/Swiss music and art and their connection to literary texts in German-speaking cultures. Discussion, writing assignments, and most reading assignments in German. Requisites: Pre- or coreq: GER 311 .

title: Special Studies: [Topic].  1-5 Credits.
description: New topics or approaches appropriate for third-year German proficiency level. Content varies; focus may be on various aspects of German language, literature, or culture and civilization. Requisites: Coreq: GER 311 . Repeatable 99 times when topic changes

description: Extensive practice in effective oral communication with emphasis on correct pronunciation. Reading of the play and scene rehearsals in class; public performance at end of term. Requisites: Prereq: GER 203 or equivalent. Repeatable 99 times

description: Presents the theory and practice of translation and other transformation media (e.g., the sister arts, literature into film). Repeatable 99 times when topic changes

title: Gender, Class, Race, and Nation: [Topic]
description: Part of 3-course graduate core series "Paradigms of Interpretation." Covers a range of socially embedded factors (gender, class, race, and/or nation) bearing on the interpretation of texts. Addresses ideas about identity and difference as they contribute to establishing nations, territories, borders, human hierarchies, and notions of the other. Students read texts in philosophy, literary theory, theories of race, gender, class, and/or nationality, in conjunction with German literary and artistic texts. Repeatable 2 times for a maximum of 12 credits when topic changes

title: Subject, Consciousness, Mind: [Topic]
description: Part of a three-term series: "Paradigms of Interpretation." Deals with questions of consciousness, subjectivity, and mind in modernist philosophy and criticism. Focus is both on philosophical method and on methodology in/for the humanities and arts in first half of 20th century. Students will read texts in philosophy, psychoanalysis, literary theory, and theory of race and gender, in conjunction with German literary and artistic texts. Repeatable 2 times for a maximum of 12 credits when topic changes

title: Photography, Film, Video, Electronic Media: [Topic]
description: Part of 3-term series "Mediations." With a focus on photography, film, video, and/or electronic media, this course traces the aesthetic and cultural significance of those media for understanding the ways that technologies of reproduction reconfigure the relation between media and literature, politics and aesthetics, mass culture and high art in German culture, aesthetics, and literature. Repeatable 2 times for a maximum of 12 credits when topic changes

title: Environment and Text: [Topic]
description: Part of 3-term series "Mediations." With a focus on literature, painting, philosophy and critical theory, this course will examine how German writers, artists, philosophers, and theorists have processed often dramatic changes in the conception of nature in relation to human life and thought. Repeatable 2 times for a maximum of 12 credits when topic changes

title: Translations Transformations: [Topic]
description: Already exists: Presents the theory and practice of translation and other transformation media (e.g., the sister arts, literature into film). Repeatable when topic changes. Part of 3-term series "Mediations." Repeatable 2 times for a maximum of 12 credits when topic changes

title: Introduction to Global Issues
description: Survey of major political, economic, and cultural themes in international studies through in-class debates on key contemporary issues.

title: Foundations for Intercultural Competence
description: This course teaches practical skills and analytic frameworks that support safe, respectful, enlightening experiences of intercultural engagement.

title: Global Wellbeing
description: Interdisciplinary introduction to human wellbeing, focusing on health, education, and the environment and the ways they impact wellbeing. Topics will be framed by the larger issue of whether or not something called "wellbeing" is a human universal, as well as how wellbeing can be improved.

title: Perspectives on International Development
description: Introduction to major ideologies, theories, historical processes, and contemporary challenges in international development. Galvan.

title: Value Systems in Cross-Cultural Perspective
description: Introduction to value systems of various cultures, focusing on how values relate to religion, forms of social organization, group affiliation, and patterns of conflict resolution.

title: Globalization and the Global Economy
description: Introduction to the study of globalization and the global economy, analyzing ways the global economy is structured and maintained through various political interventions and regimes. This course aims to provide tools for understanding and explaining these structures and their impacts.

title: Special Studies: [Topic].  1-5 Credits.
description: Experimental course - topic varies. Repeatable 99 times

title: Global Health and Development
description: Introduction to major issues in global health, their causes and possible solutions, with a focus on the poor in developing countries.

title: African Contemporary Issues and Concerns
description: Introduces students to current challenges facing African peoples today. Extends survey of Africa courses, and prepares students for more advanced study regarding the African continent.

title: Education and Development
description: This course will introduce students to the foundational ideas in the field of international and comparative education, and help build a theoretical toolkit of the major approaches scholars utilize when analyzing education from a global perspective.

title: International Cooperation and Conflict
description: Utilizes case studies and selected themes to examine the root causes, stakeholder perspectives, and attempts to resolve international conflicts.

title: International Human Rights
description: Survey of human rights, examining diverse perspectives on the concept, practice, and implementation of human rights and human rights regimes.

title: The Global Story of Race
description: Working from a historical and cultural perspective, this course uses global case studies to explore how race came to be a key principle of social organization around the world through colonialism and extractive capitalism, and how it manifests today.

title: Global Community Development
description: Introduction to communitarian theory and local-level grass-roots development practices. Comparison across North-South divide of efforts to alleviate poverty, promote sustainability, and ensure mobilization and cohesion. Requisites: Prereq: GLBL 240 .

title: Gender and International Development
description: Analysis of gendered dynamics of changing roles, opportunities, and expectations as societies undergo social and environmental change associated with economic, social, cultural and political development.

title: Aid to Developing Countries
description: Examines the history and current dynamics of international bilateral and multilateral development assistance, the possibilities and constraints of aid, and other related issues. Requisites: Prereq: GLBL 240 .

title: United Nations Intervention in Global Crises
description: Explores the theory and practice of humanitarian aid, peace-building, and development during or after violent conflict. Focuses on work of international organizations in conflict areas or on issues of conflict.

title: Global Food Security
description: Explores explanations for, and solutions to, persistent inequities in food access. Considers the political, agricultural, economic and humanitarian aspects of the global food system.

title: Cross-Cultural Communication
description: Focuses on skills and insights needed by everyone working in cross-cultural settings. Considers values, development, education, politics, and environment as central to cross-cultural understanding. Theoretical as well as applied approach, with lectures, discussions and hands-on activities.

title: Global Perspectives on Disability
description: This class uses a human rights paradigm to examine issues facing people with disabilities throughout the world. Readings and discussions will emphasize cross-disability and cross-cultural approaches to gender and disability, international development and disability, inclusive educational models, and cross-cultural aspects of disability.

title: South Asia: Development and Social Change
description: Introduction to the vast social changes and development issues confronting the South Asian subcontinent.

title: Europe Development and Social Change
description: Through the lens of development studies, students (1) develop a fundamental knowledge and literacy regarding current European quality of life issues, (2) critically examine how these issues are viewed by various stakeholders, and (3) consider attempts to enhance human, national and regional development processes.

title: Southeast Asia Today
description: Introduction to mainland and island Southeast Asia, with a special focus on social changes that are occurring across the region and how change is affecting peoples and societies.

title: Development and Social Change in Sub-Saharan Africa
description: Introduces theoretical and practical aspects of development and social change in sub-Saharan Africa, with focus on key issues in African development during the post-colonial era.

title: Development and Social Change in Latin America
description: Explores development challenges, debt cycles, urban growth, neoliberalism, populism, socialism, gender, the environment, U.S.–Latin American relations, ecotourism, and drug geographies in the region.

title: Bollywood's Lens on Indian Society
description: Explores Indian society through film, focusing on critical social issues; depicted vs. the historical reality; and ongoing transformations of social orientations and values.

title: Population Displacement and Global Health
description: Explores health and mental health problems affecting displaced (migrant and refugee) communities and considers underdevelopment as a fundamental cause of displacement and health problems. Offered once per academic year.

title: Global Reproductive Health
description: Overview of issues in global reproductive health, including politics, economics, historical and cultural factors. Implications for international health and development programs reviewed. Offered alternate years.

title: Scholarly and Professional Texts
description: Students will acquire skills and apply strategies to improve graduate-level reading effectiveness and comprehension by identifying the type of text, purpose for reading, rhetorical structures and features, and active vocabulary building. Field research into the types of rhetorical styles encountered in academic disciplines and matching appropriate reading strategies per style will be included. While this class targets international and non-native English-speaking students, any graduate student is welcome to join.

title: Reading for the Legal Profession
description: This course is designed to assist international students in the LLM program to understand the structure and cultural and institutional expectations of graduate-level law degree programs. It will also assist students in acquiring specialized legal vocabulary, recognizing and comprehending legal rhetorical styles and texts, and developing reading skills specific to the legal profession. Concurrent enrollment in the LLM Fundamentals of American Legal Systems course required.

title: Writing for the Legal Profession
description: This course is designed to support international LLM students in comprehending legal rhetorical styles and formats, and in acquiring the skills required to write a variety of legal texts that are accurate and which adhere to specialized legal writing conventions. Target texts include various types of briefs, motions, and memoranda. Concurrent enrollment in the LLM Comparative Legal Systems course required.

title: Master of Laws Reading
description: This course is focused on increasing international students' comprehension and retention of key course concepts, essential terms, and reading assignments in the LLM course Fundamentals of American Legal Systems. Students will use reading and vocabulary acquisition strategies and be provided opportunities to ask questions and clarify meaning of course concepts and terms in order to improve their ability to participate successfully in the LLM companion course.

title: Master of Laws Writing
description: This course is focused on increasing international students' ability to complete writing assignments successfully in the LLM course Comparative Legal Systems. Multiple drafts, grammar instruction, and peer and instructor feedback and student self-assessment help students gain the knowledge and skills they need to produce the high-quality writing required in the companion law course.

title: Food and Culture in the Middle East
description: Getting to know Middle Eastern countries through food is one of the marvelous ways to provide a peerless connection with these unique cultures.

title: Introduction to Epidemiology
description: This course provides an introduction to the basic principles and methods of epidemiology. Epidemiology is the study of the distribution and determinants of health and disease in populations, and it is considered a cornerstone of public health. Topics covered include study designs, infectious disease epidemiology and pandemics, and chronic disease, environmental, and social epidemiology. This course will strengthen your ability to think critically about population health, with an eye toward health equity and justice.

title: Special Studies: [Topic].  1-5 Credits.

title: Global Perspectives in Food and Film
description: In this class we will highlight the power of documentary films to disseminate information about food systems, sustainability, and health in our media-saturated world. Using food as a lens, we will critically examine films within their social, cultural, and global contexts.

title: History and Ethics of Global Health
description: This course examines the ethics of global health from an interdisciplinary liberal arts perspective, with a particular attention to the history of this field. It is broad in geographic and chronological scope, beginning with ancient medical codes such as the Hippocratic Oath. A bulk of our time will be spent from 1900 to the present when the antecedents of global health (tropical medicine, colonial medicine, international medicine) emerged.

title: First-Year Modern Hebrew I
description: This is the first course in the Hebrew courses sequence. This course is designed for students with no previous knowledge of the language. In this class, you will engage in readings and writing using Modern Hebrew script, vocabulary related to the immediate environment, basic grammar, and basic morphological structure. Only Verbs in the present tense (First Pattern) will be taught in this course. Conducted in Hebrew. Sequence with HBRW 102 , HBRW 103 .

title: First-Year Modern Hebrew II
description: This is the second course in the Hebrew courses sequence.  This course is designed for students who successfully passed ( HBRW 101 ) or have been placed in ( HBRW 102 ) after completing the Hebrew placement test. In this class, you will continue developing your communication skills, expanding your vocabulary in Hebrew, and gaining more understanding of Israeli culture. You will read, write, and speak about everyday topics, using different verbs, identifying roots, and recognizing a few Hebrew verb patterns. Conducted in Hebrew. Sequence with HBRW 101 , HBRW 103 . Requisites: Prereq: HBRW 101 .

title: First-Year Modern Hebrew III
description: This is the third course in the Hebrew courses sequence. This course is designed for students who successfully passed ( HBRW 102 ) or have been placed in ( HBRW 103 ) after completing the Hebrew placement test. In this class, you will continue developing your communication skills, expanding your vocabulary in Hebrew, and gaining more understanding of Israeli culture. You will read, write, and speak by practicing present tense from different patterns and using the imperative. Conducted in Hebrew. Sequence with HBRW 101 , HBRW 102 . Requisites: Prereq: HBRW 102 .

title: Special Studies: [Topic].  1-12 Credits.
description: Experimental course - topic varies. Repeatable 99 times

title: Second-Year Modern Hebrew I
description: This is the fourth course in the Hebrew courses sequence. This course is designed for students who successfully passed ( HBRW 103 ) or have been placed in ( HBRW 301 ) after completing the Hebrew placement test. In this class, you will continue developing your communication skills, expanding your grammatical knowledge, and gaining more understanding of Israeli culture. You will discover the past tense from the first pattern and more verb forms in the present tense. You will also learn essential subordinating conjunctions, along with the morphological structures of verbs. Conducted in Hebrew. Sequence with HBRW 302 , HBRW 303 . Requisites: Prereq: HBRW 103 .

title: Second-Year Modern Hebrew II
description: This is the fifth course in the Hebrew Courses sequence. This course is designed for students who successfully passed ( HBRW 301 ) or have been placed in ( HBRW 302 ) after completing the Hebr.ew placement test. In this course, you will learn more about the different verbal patterns in the past tense and more complex grammatical structures such as the construct state. This course will also be focused on significantly improving your speaking and comprehension skills. Conducted in Hebrew. Sequence with HBRW 301 , HBRW 303 . Requisites: Prereq: HBRW 301 .

title: Second-Year Modern Hebrew III
description: This is the sixth course in the Hebrew Courses sequence. This course is designed for students who successfully passed ( HBRW 302 ) or have been placed in ( HBRW 303 ) after completing the Hebrew placement test. In the course, you will complete learning the present tense & Past tense conjugations. You will learn more complex grammatical structures such as advanced construct states and superlatives. Conducted in Hebrew. Sequence with HBRW 301 , HBRW 302 . Requisites: Prereq: HBRW 302 .

description: Topics center encountering and critically reflecting upon cultures, identities, and ways of being in global contexts beyond the United States. Each course will include substantial scholarship, cultural production, perspectives, and voices from members of communities under study, as sources permit. Repeatable 3 times for a maximum of 16 credits when topic changes

description: Offered in a range of topics with an emphasis on science. Repeatable 3 times for a maximum of 16 credits when topic changes

Repeatable 3 times for a maximum of 16 credits when topic changes

title: Thesis Prospectus
description: Students create prospectus, exchange critiques and ideas, and present research in mock defenses with thesis advisor present.

title: Making Modern Europe
description: Historical development of Europe; major changes in value systems, ideas, social structures, economic institutions, and forms of political life. From the Renaissance to Napoleon.

title: Europe and the World
description: Historical development of the Western world; major changes in value systems, ideas, social structures, economic institutions, and forms of political life. From Napoleon to the present.

title: World History Antiquity to 1500
description: Survey of world cultures and civilizations and their actions. Includes study of missionary religions, imperialism, economic and social relations. Ancient societies.

title: World History
description: Survey of world cultures and civilizations and their actions. Includes study of missionary religions, imperialism, economic and social relations. Early modern.

title: World History
description: Survey of world cultures and civilizations and their actions. Includes study of missionary religions, imperialism, economic and social relations. Modern.

title: Cultures of India
description: Introduces students to the historical study of culture in the Indian subcontinent.

title: Foundations of East Asian Civilizations
description: Introduction to traditional China and Japan; Confucianism, Buddhism, Daoism; floating worlds; family and gender; traditional views of the body; literati class; samurai; Mongols and Manchus.

title: China, Past and Present
description: Introduction to Chinese culture. Explores meanings of past and present in 20th-century efforts to modernize China. Chronological and topical inquiry into politics, literature, social structure, gender, art, economy.

title: Japan, Past and Present
description: Introduction to Japanese culture. Explores myth, tradition, modernity, and postmodernity with one eye trained on the future. Examples from personal experience.

title: Special Studies: [Topic].  1-5 Credits.
description: Problem-oriented course designed for students interested in history who might or might not become majors. Repeatable 99 times

title: Inventing America
description: Development of the North American continent socially, economically, politically, culturally. Native America, European colonization, colonial development, origins of slavery, Revolution, early Republic.

title: Building the United States
description: Creation and development of the United States and its social, economical, political, and cultural consequences. Jacksonian era, expansion, commercial and industrial revolution, slavery, Civil War, Reconstruction, Gilded Age, imperialism, and the Progressive Era.

title: American Century
description: Creation and development of the so-called "American Century" socially, economically, politically, culturally. Imperialism, progressivism, modernity, the 1920s, Depression and New Deal, world wars and Cold War, 1960s, and recent developments.

title: Food in World History
description: Surveys the development of eating practices, tastes, foodstuffs, and culinary philosophies from early human history to the present in diverse parts of the world.

title: Beer in World History
description: A wide-ranging study of beer from its origins 10,000 years ago to the present develops our sense of shared humanity at the same time as stressing local difference and historical change. Beer is rooted in local geography, environment, culture, social structure, politics and religion. It also tells a story of human ingenuity, innovation, adaptation and resilience around the world. This course offers an introduction to the intersection of science, technology and society through the lens of one of humanity's most ancient and global technologies: brewing beer.

title: War in the Modern World I
description: Evolution of the conduct of war in the 19th and 20th centuries as a reflection of social, political, and technological developments.

title: War in the Modern World II
description: Surveys changes in the nature and conduct of warfare in light of social, political, and technological developments from 1945 to present.

title: Latinos in the Americas
description: Explores historical experiences of Latino groups, emphasizing Mexican and Caribbean migrations. Lectures in English; readings and discussions in English, Spanish, and Spanglish. Three years of high school Spanish, SPAN 103 with a grade of C or better, or raised in a bilingual household recommended.

title: African American History
description: The African background, development of slavery, abolitionism, the Civil War and Reconstruction.

title: African American History
description: The 20th-century African American experience including the great migration, World War II, the Civil Rights Movement, post-1970 African America.

title: History of the Pacific Islands
description: Pacific History explores the history of peoples, islands, and oceans from New Zealand to Hawai'i and many places in between.  Covering the past 500 years, it draws together human and natural history.

title: Portland Past and Present
description: Portland is the principal urban center of Oregon, and one of the most important cities on the West Coast of the United States.  With a history that is at once inspiring and troubling, Portland is a city of contradictions. This course will introduce students to the history of this Northwest Metropolis.

title: Introduction to Global Environmental History
description: Introduction to concepts, concerns, and methods of environmental history.

title: Cities in India and South Asia
description: This course examines the economic, cultural and social dimensions of cities in the subcontinent of India with comparisons from other Asian and North American cities.

title: Historian's Craft
description: Gateway course to the History major. Explores the diverse meanings and methods of modern historical interpretation. Trains students to work with original historical sources and become more effective consumers and producers of historical writing, preparing them for upper-division coursework.

title: Modern Europe
description: Political, social, cultural, intellectual, and economic trends from the 18th century to the present. 18th century. McCole.

title: Modern Europe
description: Political, social, cultural, intellectual, and economic trends in the 19th century.

title: Modern Europe
description: Political, social, cultural, intellectual, and economic trends in the 20th century.

title: History of Women in the United States I
description: Survey of the diverse experiences of American women from 1600 to 1870.

title: History of Women in the United States II
description: Survey of the diverse experiences of American women from 1870 to present.

title: Oregon Hidden Histories
description: Lane County, home of the University of Oregon, is not known for its racial, ethnic, and religious diversity. Yet indigenous inhabitants, white settlers, Black residents, Muslims and Jews, and immigrants and Americans with roots in every continent have made their homes here. This workshop-style class engages students in conducting original historical resources about local hidden histories, writing them into a narrative, and sharing them with the public. Students acquire research, writing, revision, and web design skills as they produce a polished web publication.

title: Migrants and Refugees in Modern World History
description: Human beings have always moved; the system of borders and passports to regulate that movement is just a few centuries old. This course gives students historical tools and reference points to help them better understand the controversial issues surrounding international migration in the world today.

title: Precolonial Africa
description: Survey of African history to the mid-19th century, analyzing processes of state formation, regional and long-distance trade, religion, oral tradition, and systems of slavery.

title: Colonial and Postcolonial Africa
description: Survey of African history from the late 1800s to the turn of the 21st century. Emphasis is on the internal dynamics of change as well as the effects of colonialism and global interaction.

title: US Military History
description: Survey of US military history from the colonial period to the present with a focus on the organization, operations, and strategy of the US Army in wartime.

title: German History: [Topic]
description: Middle Ages to the end of the 20th century. I: Middle Ages and Reformation (1410–1648). II: Germany in the Old Regime and Age of Revolution (1648–1848). III: Modern Germany (1848–present). Repeatable 2 times for a maximum of 12 credits when topic changes

title: Imperial Russia
description: Siberian and North American expansion; Peter the Great; Catherine the Great; abolition of serfdom; industrialization; Silver Age culture and revolution; World War I and collapse.

title: Soviet Union and Contemporary Russia
description: Examines the rise, development, and collapse of the Soviet Union, the world's first communist regime. Topics include the Russian Revolution, Stalinism, war, culture, and society.

title: American Radicalism
description: Motives, strategies, successes, and failures of radical movements and their significance for American society. Workers' movements, socialism, communism, African American freedom struggle, nationalist movements of people of color, feminism, student activism.

title: The United States in the 1960s
description: Exploration of a watershed era: civil rights, student activism, educational crisis, Vietnam War, gender revolution, environmentalism.

title: Early Modern Science
description: Explores the emergence of experimental science in early modern Europe and its interaction with larger social, political, and cultural issues, such as gender, race, colonialism and capitalism.

title: History of US Cities
description: Course introduces students to the history of one of the most fascinating and contradictory social forms of the modern world. Students learn about the cities of the United States from a variety of perspectives, including urban planning, power and politics, and segregation and inequality.

title: United States Business History
description: American businesses from their colonial origins to the present. Interaction between the political, social, economic, and ideological environment and the internal structure and activities of business enterprises.

title: American West in Popular Culture
description: Examines the idea of the West in the American imagination as expressed in popular literature, captivity narratives, dime novels, travel literature, art, Wild West shows, films, and television.

title: American Environmental History to 1890
description: Considers how humans and their natural environments have interacted and reshaped each other through time from 1491 to 1890.

title: American Environmental History 1890 to Present
description: Focuses on environmental change and the rise of environmental politics from the Progressive Era to the present.

title: Latin America
description: Major economic, political, and cultural trends and continuities. Pre-Columbian and Iberian history, the colonial period up to 1750.

title: Latin America
description: Major economic, political, and cultural trends and continuities. Transition from late colonial mercantilism to political independence and national definition, 1750–1910.

title: Latin America, 1910 to the Present
description: A survey of major economic, political, social, and cultural changes in Latin America since 1910.

title: Soccer and Society in Latin America
description: Exploring the complexities of Latin American societies using soccer as a historical, cultural, and sociological window to issues of race, class, gender, and national identity.

title: India
description: This course will survey the history of the Indian subcontinent as both a colony of Britain and then as a cluster of independent countries in the 20th and 21st centuries.

title: Early China
description: Survey from the beginnings to the 10th century focuses on the development of Chinese thought and religion and the growth of the imperial state and bureaucracy.

title: Vietnam War and the United States
description: Tells the story of the war from the Vietnamese perspective, explores how the war reshaped American society and politics, and examines how the war has been remembered in popular culture.

title: Samurai in Film
description: Examination of the image of Japan's warrior class, the most prominent social group in Japan for over seven centuries. Combines films, readings, and lectures.

title: Workshop: [Topic].  1-12 Credits.
description: Current topics include Southeast Asia Interpretations. Repeatable 99 times

description: A presentation incorporating the knowledge and skills acquired from course work completed for a degree. Repeatable 99 times
